folkarts.name: Therukoothu | தெ ருக்கூத்த
folkarts.desc: Translating to "Street Theatre," this is a highly stylized, multi-disciplinary folk performance combining acting, singing, dance, and vibrant makeup.

frame1.modalContent: Performers wear elaborate wooden ornaments known as bujai kattai (shoulder pieces) and large, gilded headgear decorated with small mirrors.

Actors wear ornaments that give them a distinctive, square-like silhouette, signifying their non-human or heroic status. Makeup is bold and symbolic, often using a white base with fierce red and black strokes to denote specific character archetypes.
Shri Bavaani Amman Therukkoothu Nataka Manram, Kanchipuram, presented "Alamugasooran Samhaaram", an unusual story of Arjuna being helped by Draupadi, who takes on the Kali avatara to kill the asura.

While traditionally a male-dominated art form where men performed female roles (sthreepart), modern troupes have increasingly begun to include female performers, reflecting shifting social dynamics within rural Tamil Nadu.

frame1.alt: A recipient of the Bismillah Khan Yuva Puraskar
frame1.modalTitle: A recipient of the Bismillah Khan Yuva Puraskar
frame1.modalContent: Mr. Murugan was trained under the legendary Purisai Kannappa Thambiran. His career spans traditional village performances and global collaborations (e.g., France’s Théâtre du Soleil), redefining Therukoothu for international audiences.
